questions.text: Who is the author of 'The Portrait of a Lady'?
questions.explanation: The story is an autobiographical account by Khushwant Singh.

questions.text: How did the author describe his grandmother's face?
questions.explanation: She was old, and her face was covered in wrinkles running from everywhere to everywhere.

questions.text: The grandmother always carried a ______ in her hand.
questions.explanation: One hand rested on her waist while the other told the beads of her rosary.

questions.text: Where did the author's parents leave him when they went to live in the city?
questions.explanation: The author lived with his grandmother in the village while his parents settled in the city.

questions.text: What would the grandmother give the author for breakfast in the village?
options: Stale chapattis with butter and sugar
questions.explanation: She would prepare a thick, stale chapatti with a little butter and sugar spread on it.

questions.text: Why did the grandmother accompany the author to school in the village?
options: The school was attached to the temple
She wanted to meet the priest
questions.explanation: She would sit inside the temple reading scriptures while he was in class.

questions.text: Who did the grandmother feed on their way back from the village school?
questions.explanation: She would throw the stale chapattis to the village dogs.

questions.text: The turning point in their friendship came when they:
questions.explanation: Moving to the city changed their routine and limited their interaction.

questions.text: In the city, the author went to an English school by:
questions.explanation: He no longer walked with her; he took a motor bus.

questions.text: Why was the grandmother distressed by the author's English school education?
options: There was no teaching about God and scriptures
questions.explanation: She believed education should include spiritual and religious teachings.

questions.text: What was the grandmother's opinion on music lessons at the English school?
options: She thought it was for harlots and beggars
questions.explanation: She associated music with 'lewd association' and not for 'gentlefolk'.

questions.text: When the author went to University, the common link of their friendship was:
questions.explanation: He was given a separate room, and the remaining common link was broken.

questions.text: How did the grandmother spend her afternoons in the city?
questions.explanation: She would sit in the veranda and break bread into little bits for hundreds of little birds.

questions.text: The 'happiest half-hour of the day' for the grandmother was when she:
questions.explanation: Feeding the birds brought her the most joy in her secluded life.

questions.text: When the author was going abroad, the grandmother was:
questions.explanation: She saw him off at the station but showed no emotion, continuing her prayers.

questions.text: The grandmother's last physical sign of affection for the author at the station was:
questions.explanation: She silently kissed his forehead as he left for five years.

questions.text: What did the grandmother do to celebrate the author's return from abroad?
options: Collected women from the neighborhood and sang
questions.explanation: She got an old drum and sang of the homecoming of warriors for several hours.

questions.text: The first time the grandmother forgot to pray was when:
questions.explanation: During the evening of the author's return, she omitted her prayers for the first time.

questions.text: The grandmother predicted her end by saying:
options: She was ready to go to the city
questions.explanation: She realized she was dying because she had missed her prayers.

questions.text: During her last moments, the grandmother:
questions.explanation: She refused to talk and spent her last moments telling the beads of her rosary.

questions.text: How did the family know the grandmother was dead?
options: The rosary fell from her lifeless fingers
questions.explanation: Her lips stopped moving and the rosary fell, marking her passing.

questions.text: How did the sparrows react to the grandmother's death?
options: They sat scattered on the floor in silence
questions.explanation: Thousands of sparrows sat in the veranda but did not chirp or eat.

questions.text: What did the sparrows do when the grandmother's corpse was carried off?
questions.explanation: They flew away quietly, showing they were mourning her death.

questions.text: The author's grandmother was like the 'winter landscape in the mountains' because of her:
options: Serene and calm appearance in white
questions.explanation: She was an expanse of pure white serenity, breathing peace and contentment.

questions.text: What was the author's grandfather wearing in the portrait?
options: A big turban and loose-fitting clothes
questions.explanation: His portrait showed him with a long white beard and a big turban.

questions.text: The author thought the idea of his grandmother being young and pretty was:
questions.explanation: He could only imagine her as old and wrinkled, never young.

questions.text: The grandmother used to wake the author up and get him ready for:
questions.explanation: She prepared him for village school and sang her morning prayers.

questions.text: Why did the author listen to the grandmother's morning prayers?
questions.explanation: He loved her voice but never bothered to learn the words.

questions.text: What did the grandmother use to plaster the author's wooden slate?
questions.explanation: She used yellow chalk to prepare his slate for the day.

questions.text: The village school priest taught the children the alphabet and the:
questions.explanation: The children sat in rows in the veranda and sang the prayer in a chorus.

questions.text: How many years was the author abroad for his studies?
questions.explanation: He was abroad for five years for higher studies.

questions.text: When the grandmother died, the sweepers swept the bread crumbs into the:
questions.explanation: The sparrows ignored the crumbs, and the sweeper swept them into the dustbin the next morning.

questions.text: The grandmother's silver locks were scattered ______ over her pale face.
questions.explanation: Her white hair (silver locks) was untidily scattered on her face.

questions.text: In the city, the grandmother sat by her ______ from sunrise to sunset.
questions.explanation: She spent most of her time spinning and reciting prayers.

questions.text: What did the grandmother do when she stopped the music celebration?
options: She fell ill with a mild fever
questions.explanation: The next morning she was taken ill with a mild fever after overstraining herself.

questions.text: According to the grandmother, music was the monopoly of:
questions.explanation: She had strong traditional views on what music was intended for.

questions.text: The author's parents were successfully ______ in the city.
questions.explanation: They called for the author and grandmother once they were settled.

questions.text: The sparrows took no notice of the ______ thrown by the author's mother.
questions.explanation: They were mourning the grandmother and didn't touch the bread crumbs.

questions.text: The grandmother was always hobbling about the house in ______ white.
questions.explanation: She wore spotless white clothes, adding to her serene appearance.

questions.text: How did the grandmother feel when she couldn't help the author with his city school lessons?
questions.explanation: She was unhappy that she could not help him with things like English and Western Science.
